sentences.text: [ELECTRIC TONE]
My name is Jonathan Dresner, J-O-N-A-T-H-A-N, D-R-E-S-N-E-R.
My nickname or my name that I was use at home is Janek.
J-A-N-E-K.
I was born in Cracow, in Poland.
On September the 4th, 1923.
72 and half.
I was 16 when the-- when the war broke out.
I was a student in the high school.
I was attending the Hebrew school, from the very beginning.
I went to the Hebrew school as elementary school, then to the high school, which was part of-- the second part of the elementary school.
It was a private Jewish Hebrew school in Cracow.
We had been living in a region which was mostly-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where mostly Jews where we living, although it was not the real ghetto of Cracow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All my friends were Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have no contact with Poles, whatsoever, until the war broke out.
All my friends, all my teachers, all my surroundings were Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- I came from a family-- I come from a family which wasn't very religious.
But I should say we were traditional.
We have been belonging to an orthodox synagogue, but-- and observing Shabbat.
But not to-- nothing very special, more.
I have a very, very comfortable and good childhood.
I had-- I was living in a very comfortable conditions.
My parents were very wealthy.
And if you think about the conditions from before the war, in Poland, so we were living in a-- as children, I and my sister, in very comfortable and good conditions.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we-- we didn't feel anything that-- bothered us, until the-- until the war broke out.
We have been meeting friends.
We are very busy, because, first of all, because it was a Hebrew school.
So we learned both-- Polish and secular and Hebrew subjects.
There were four Hebrew subjects, which were-- were additional to the normal subjects, which the Poles-- because we're obliged to learn.
By the way, the matriculation, the end of the school, we needed to make two-- two kind of exams, one in Polish and one in Hebrew.
So our school lasted longer than normal Polish schools.
So we came home later than Polish kids.
Then we had much more homework to do than the Polish kids, again, because of the two languages we have been taught.
Then we met with the-- we have been most of us hand some some learning about the language, or music, or something like this It was a-- it was additional occupation.
Then we met, and played, and met-- had friends.
Vacations, we went mostly with the family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not far from Cracow.
And mostly, we stayed-- if you speak about vacations during the summer, you-- the vacations were two months.
And we stayed mostly, the whole two months, in someplace, which-- at least with my mother.
Fathers were mostly coming at weekends to join the families.
And as I told you, we have been in rather good conditions, so mostly the nanny or somebody was coming with us, to help the mother, as long as we were [INAUDIBLE].
He had a-- a big fabric store, which was partially wholesale and partially retail.
And again, speaking about the conditions from before the war in Cracow, it was rather a big business.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if you can compare it with something, but in those times, when somebody employed 27 people, it was [INAUDIBLE] it is a rather big business.
Yeah, all the time, full time, with-- with my father.
That's one of the reasons that I-- I saw my parents rather rarely.
They were coming, very late, home, coming at noon to-- to have their lunch for a very short time.
So we were raised almost without parents, during the days.
She's much younger, but she-- she began at the same school, yeah.
I told you already, because I have no contact with Poles.
So I-- I can't say that I have.
It was before the-- shortly before the war break out, and I was already 16.
We have been working [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] more in the regions where the Poles were gathering.
So once upon a time, we met the anti-Semites, and even got some beatings from them.
But this were-- was very rare.
And the reason was that I-- I wasn't coming, too much, together with Poles.
We have to remember that Cracow was conquered, if I am not mistaken, in three days, four days after the war broke out.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] almost, I think, a week after the-- the-- the town was occupied by the Germans, they began to-- to fight against Jews and to-- to call out some special orders, how the Jews have to behave.

From the-- almost from the very first moment, I don't remember if it was a week or two or months, but it was very shortly after they came to Cracow that they made us know that we are something other than the Polish, although the Poles were occupied, like we were.
And at that time, they suffered mentally, maybe, the same way we suffered.
But we-- the Jews began to suffer, physically, quite quickly.
I don't remember exactly, but it was very short, after they occupied the town.
So it-- it began with physical [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] beatings on the street, when they met Poles-- Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after a while, they began to-- it was a very well-known play by the Germans, when they met a religious Jew who has a-- a beard and-- and--
Peyas-- so they cut them down.
It was very spectacular for them.
And they made out of it very special spectacles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never had a beard.
I never had a peyas, so I was not exposed to this.
But seeing the others, it was already-- I was already a part of this physical, let's say, torture-- or beginning of the physical torture.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was the second part.
And the third part began when they began to gather the Jewish people, in order to let them work on the streets.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a [INAUDIBLE] word that means-- it was-- in the meantime, was already cold.
You have to know that in-- Cracow was conquered in September, and in late October or in the beginning of November, there's already snow in Poland.
So the-- they gathered people to shuttle-- shuffle the snow, and which means-- not to shuffle away, to shuffle from one side of the street to the other, just in order to make the people work.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then to-- to clean the streets, then to bring things from one side of the town to the other side of the town, to schlep things from one street to another, just to make the people suffer-- so these were the first signs of-- of a very special approach to Jews.
We did-- yeah?
In the beginning, yes.
I-- as far as I remember, it took about four months that they let the Jewish students-- they allowed them to-- to go on in studying.
After, I think, four months-- I think until the-- the end of '39, maybe the beginning of '40, they stopped and closed-- first of all, closed the-- the Jewish schools.
And one of the worst-- first ones which was rolled closed was the Hebrew school.
And then they expelled every Jew-- Jewish student from-- from the Polish school, because many, many of the Jewish kids were going to-- to public schools.
The Hebrew school was a private school.
So they expelled all the children, and that was [INAUDIBLE].
This was the time of-- of general education of Jews in Poland, as far as I know.
And--
This began, also-- this began, also, quite quickly.
I don't remember exactly the-- the dates.
But it was-- the policy of Germans was the bigger the business was, too put a so-called-- they-- they called it, in German, [GERMAN].
That means a man, whom they trust, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over the-- upon the-- the-- the owner or the manager of-- of the business.
And this man was-- in the beginning, he had to control the business, and to take all the money, and to control it.

And the policy was, with the time, the same business, if it will succeed, it-- it will stop being Jewish and will belong to the-- to the Germans, through the man who was the so-called trustee, or I don't know how to call it in English.
And mostly, it didn't work too long.
It worked a year, a year and a half, or two.

Then the Germans took the whole merchandise out of it, and closed, because you have to know that at least 90%, if not more, of these [GERMAN], of these men, whom they trusted, those were the people who got it, because they were-- they were not real Germans.
They were all Ukrainians, or half-Germans, or even Poles who spied for Germany before the war.
And as they were-- as an award, as a prize, they got this Jewish property to-- to keep, to manage, to keep, and to steal.
It was all together.
They had a wonderful life.
Almost 100% of them didn't come from the town they were-- they were put.
We had-- the first one we had was a Ukrainian who was called, so-called, Volksdeutsche.
Volksdeutsche means a half-German.
That means a man who declares himself being German.
He wasn't.
He-- he knew German very, very badly.
And he needed my father to-- to-- to contact him with the Germans, when they came into the-- into the business.
So in the very beginning, he was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was a manager on the-- but only, he kept himself inside, because he know-- he knew his limitations.
All he wanted is to have a very good life, to spend a lot of money, to earn a lot of money, which [INAUDIBLE], because all the money went to him.
And he stole 80% out of it.
He didn't give it back.
And so there was a kind of cooperation, because he needed my-- my father more than my father needed him.
After a year-- I don't remember, exactly, after a year or something, he was sent away.

And then we had another one, who was already a German, a real German, because the businesses who-- who were left got-- after a year, the Germans saw that all these people are-- are thieves, and they cannot rely on them.
So they changed them for-- for-- but these were already-- only the businesses were left, and they were not too many.
Ours was left because, as I told you, it was rather a big one.
And by the way, we-- we-- have been-- you know Cracow was-- was the capital of the German occupied Poland, because Warsaw was ruined, almost 100%.
So Cracow was the capital.
And in the castle of Cracow was reigning the so-called governor, general governor of-- of Poland, who was Mr.
Frank.
And our-- our-- or my father's business, they all came to my father's business to buy-- so-called-- to buy the fabrics.
So we were left longer than the others.
And maybe because of this, we got, afterwards, German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].
And your parents and the rest of the employees continued to work there?
The first time, all of them were working.

Even I was working there, because after the school was interrupted, everybody had to had-- had to had a job, because then, if you hadn't a job, he was caught, almost every day, to some work, like I told you before, to clean the streets and so on.
So I was inscribed as a-- a employee in my father's.
And I really went there, at least a few times a week.
And-- but it was only the first time.
Then they shortened it.
At the end, only my father and some were left.
And then, with time, nobody was left.
It was also a very, very special policy of the Germans.
First of all, they-- they throw out every Jewish family from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from a house.
It means-- these were all rented flats.
Almost 100% percent of the Jews were living in rented flats.
If the flat and-- or the house was a beautiful one, and a comfortable one, and well-equipped, and furnitured, so the chances were the-- I think it was, again, three, four months, maybe even earlier.
After the Germans came to Cracow, all these Jews were thrown out of their houses.
And the Germans got them, because they-- as it was the capital, there were a lot of Germans, which came to Cracow, to live there as a governor employees, as-- as all possible thing.
So they needed the-- the best way was to get the beautiful houses of the wealthy Jews, especially.
So a-- as I told you before, we were rather wealthy, and we had a very, very nice apartment.
I-- at that time, I didn't realize how nice it was.
But then I understood it.
And so we were one of-- of the first who-- who-- where the Germans-- it was the whole house.
It was the house of, if I'm not mistaken, 12 inhabitants, 12 flats were in there.
You mean it's a whole-- it's a whole building.
The whole building, yeah, the whole building-- it was four floors, and very comfortable.
So the Germans came and threw out every Jew.
And to our-- to our place came a couple.
He was something, a big shot in political career.
I don't know exactly how to explain it, what he was.
But he was a rather politically, and not-- but he was sent to Cracow to-- to-- it was a young couple, married, I think, half a year, a year before.
And after, maybe-- they came in, and we had to get out a day after.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So my mother, I don't know why she made it.
But it was-- it was rather spontaneously.
She asked him, maybe-- we had a-- a-- a very big flat, apartment.
So she said, maybe you want us to stay, one room.
And we'll be your servants.
Maybe it will give you a chance to-- to have a good housekeepers in-- so he said, not a bad idea.
I will try it, but I don't know if I'm allowed.
But as he was-- afterwards, we knew he was a big-shot, politically.
So he-- how he got the permission or he took the permission, I don't know what was.
After a few hours, he came.
You can stay.
So we stayed, all of us, in the bedroom of my parents.

And afterwards, we saw that we were very lucky, because first of all, we were left in a very good region, because the other had to-- to go over into the places where-- where the life, before ghetto, it was at least a year and a half before we were picked in ghetto.
The conditions were much worse.
So we were-- first, we stayed in our house.
Then the sanitation was much better.
Everything was much better.
So we liked it.
And with the time-- it's a very interesting chapter of our life, because with the time, we got so friendly with these Germans that we are listening-- I am jumping.
After half a year or something of-- first of all, we were mixing the-- in the whole flat.
I was sleeping in my room, after a while.
And we were going around.
It means we didn't go to their bedroom.
And their bedroom was established in my sister's room, which was my sister's room before.
But we were, very often, sitting in the dining room all together.
And we got so friendly that-- I don't remember, but we-- we came, even, to such a point that we were listening to English radio together, which was something outstanding, and-- and really rare.
And I jump again, in my story.
This man, some-- I don't know if-- how-- how he changed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He changed so much that he-- with time, the persecution of Jews went on and on.
And it was worse and worse.
So he came to my father, and--
And asked him to let me-- he wanted to take me to Turkey, to get me out, because I-- I was a young-- the young one, so he wanted to take me out of Poland, to bring me to Turkey.
And then I can go wherever I want.
It was not accepted.
By all of us-- first of all, we didn't believe.
The secondly, we were afraid.
Second-- and thirdly, don't forget, Jews were always hoping it's last month, or the last week, or the last day of the-- but a fact is that we came to such relations that he could speak about it.
And at the end, I don't remember.
It was maybe after a year.
Maybe he spoke too much.

He was talking too much, or maybe he was even discussing the Jewish problems when he was where he worked, or-- at the end, he was taken to the-- to the army and-- as a punishment-- and sent to the Russian front.
And he was killed, and we know that he was killed.
Before he-- before he-- they left, his wife went back to Germany.
And he was sent to-- to the army.
Before they left, they said, we will say-- we will settle another couple, which is at least as good as we are.
And it was really so.
They sent us a-- somebody who was a very nice person.
He came, not with his wife, with his mistress, but--
